intro:
The number forty appears so often at the great turning points of Scripture that it functions as a symbol in its own right: it marks a complete season of testing, trial, and discipline — long enough to prove and purify — after which God brings his servant or his people through to a new beginning. Whenever a forty appears, the reader is being told to watch for probation followed by transition.

The pattern is set in the days of Noah, when the rain fell "forty days and forty nights" and the old world was washed away to make room for a new one (Genesis 7:12). It deepens with Moses, who is on Mount Sinai with God "forty days and forty nights," neither eating nor drinking, before the covenant is given (Exodus 24:18; Exodus 34:28). The spies search the land for forty days, and for their unbelief Israel must wander "forty years in the wilderness" — a year for each day — until a faithless generation passes and a new one enters the promise (Numbers 14:33–34). Moses later names the purpose of those years: "that he might humble you, testing you to know what was in your heart" (Deuteronomy 8:2).

The same rhythm sounds across the Old Testament. Elijah, fed by an angel, travels "forty days and forty nights" to meet God at Horeb (1 Kings 19:8). Goliath taunts Israel for forty days before deliverance comes (1 Samuel 17:16). Jonah preaches that in "forty days Nineveh shall be overthrown," granting the city a season to repent (Jonah 3:4). Again and again, forty is the measured space God gives for testing, warning, or preparation.

All these gather to a point in Jesus Christ. Led by the Spirit into the wilderness, he fasts "forty days and forty nights" and is tempted by the devil (Matthew 4:1–2; Mark 1:13). Where Israel tested God through forty years and fell, the true Son endures forty days and stands firm, answering every temptation with the very words of Deuteronomy that Israel had failed to keep. He passes the test the old covenant people could not. After his resurrection he appears to the disciples over "forty days," instructing them about the kingdom of God before his ascension and the new age of the Spirit (Acts 1:3).

Forty matters because it teaches the shape of God's dealings with his people: he does not bring us to glory without a wilderness. Testing is not God's rejection but his refining, a bounded season with an appointed end. And the comfort of the motif is Christ, who walked the wilderness of forty days in our place and came through victorious, so that those who are in him may pass through their own seasons of trial toward the promise that surely lies on the other side.
